paragraphs: Michael K. Romano  https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7457-4398 Notes 1. While President Obama did nominate Merrick Garland to fill Justice Scalia's seat in 2016, since his nomination was blocked by the Senate, he was never able to present himself before the Judiciary Committee. 2. We recognize that language choice in discussions of sex and gender are nuanced and acknowledge the limitations to the way in which terms like "man," "woman," "male," and "female" impose on the nonbinary fluidity of gender identity. Solely for conceptual clarity, and following scholarship from Bos and her colleagues (Bos et al. 2022), we use the term sex in this paper in reference to more binary measures that distinguish between men and women. We treat gender as the socially and culturally imposed meaning imposed upon sex differences (McDermott 2016), which tends to impose a scale of masculinity vs. femininity when discussing socially constructed gender roles. When referencing individuals, we opt to use pronouns based on how the subject self-identifies. 3. The datasets generated and used for the corresponding analysis are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request. Please contact mromano@su.edu for data availability. 4. https://www.abalegalprofile.com/women.php#anchor2. 5. Descriptive data was collected from the American Constitution Society: https://www.acslaw.org/judicial-nominations/ january-20-2021-snapshot-diversity-of-the-federal-bench/. 6. Another issue often pointed to for the lack of gender parity in the US legal profession is the "pipeline" issue-wherein there are just. Fewer women enter into the profession than men. Escobar-Lemmon and her colleagues (2021) find little evidence that the pipeline issue has an effect on the US judiciary, however, and find more evidence that the lack of women judges may be due to systemic issues and gender bias inherent in the system of nomination and confirmation. 7. While an in-depth historical analysis of the evolution of women's entry into and fight for recognition and respect in the legal field is tempting, it is much too complicated a topic to fully discuss here. Several other scholars have, however, written at length on the topic, including Katz, Rozema, and Sanga (2023) and Scherer (2023). 8. Katz, Rozema, and Sanga (2023) note that Howard University and Boston University were East Coast schools that did admit women in the years prior to 1880. 9. It is important to note that while Reagan is pointed to as the first president to nominate a woman to the Supreme Court, he is not the first to add gender diversity to the federal bench. President Jimmy Carter made the first concerted effort to increase gender parity on the federal bench, part of a tendency for Democratic presidents to seek greater diversity in federal judgeships (Diascro and Solberg 2009;Escobar-Lemmon et al. 2021;Segal 2000;Slotnick, Goldman, and Schiavoni 2015). 10. The Smithsonian Institute notes that on the day of her nomination, the bulletin led every major television news and newspaper across the country, along with several international media outlets. Time magazine's July 20, 1981 cover featured a portrait of O'Connor with the Headline "Justice-At Last" on the front. See Thomas (2019) for details: https://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/behind-scenes- sandra-day-oconnor-first-days-supreme-court-180971441/. 11. As evident by the exchange between Senator Thurmond and Judge Scalia during the latter's opening statement, "quickly" introducing Scalia's family was "taxing" on the Judge, who had to remember all nine of his children's names. Replying to Senator Thurmond's quip that the nominee had a "good memory," Scalia responded "Just don't ask me their ages." 12. As further examples, Justice Ginsburg's opening statement offers a longer, more detailed, and personal presentation of who she is not only as a judge but also as a person. Like O'Connor, Ginsburg expresses immense gratitude to the committee throughout her statement, giving off the impression of "friendliness," a personality trait that is more common for females than males to carry due to gender norms. Ginsburg also expands on O'Connor's initial discussion of the "glass ceiling" for women in the law, touching on the efforts made by women to break the stereotypes and succeed in such a profession. These are two aspects that male nominees rarely tend to highlight. 13. Historically, formal hearings and opening statements by the nominee (and appearances of the nominee at all) were not common until Justice O'Connor's 1981 nomination (for a historical discussion of this trend, see Collins and Ringhand 2016;McMillion 2021). Thus, our data contains the current universe of opening statements made by Supreme Court nominees openly available. 14. As is the norm in topic model equations, we first pre-process the statements following a standard set of procedures (Roberts, Steward, and Tingley 2019), including tokenization, standardization of words such as lower casing, removing stopwords (terms that provide little information to the topic such as "the," "a," and "is"), and stemming/ lemmatization, which reduces terms down to their base or root to consolidate similar words to help with grouping (e.g., "running" and "run" are both lemmatized to "run" as these terms relate to the same action). 15. The number of topics is not easily deduced in a large corpus of text, so methodologists have developed several ways to determine the optimal number of topics contained within a series of documents. We follow Romano and Curry (2020) in combining multiple techniques to determine the optimal number of topics, which we determine to be three as described further in the text. Details on our measurement strategy can be provided by the authors upon request. 16. It is important to note that due to the small number of female nominees that have presented themselves in front of the Senate Judiciary Committee, the data for these structural topic models is limited. These findings, however, make intuitive sense, and we believe warrant further examination in future research by expanding the pool of opening statement to nominees for the appellate and district courts when available. 17. It is often the case that lower court confirmations eschew opening statements by both the committee and the nominee beyond simple statements of thanks before moving on to the question-and-answer session.
